properties.evidence: There has been recent controversy over the question of whether statins cause type 2 diabetes.
There are questions about a possible link of statins to pancreatitis although a recent review disputes this claim.
The authors concluded that high dose statins were significantly better than moderate dose, primarily by reducing non-fatal cardiac events.
The investigators used intravascular ultrasound to document atheroma regression after treatment with 40 mg daily of rosuvastatin.
The study authors concluded that lowering LDL-C below current recommended guidelines can cause regression of atherosclerosis in coronary disease patients.
It found that lowering LDL-C to 80 mg/dL in the treatment group was superior in preventing CVD outcomes than lowering it to 100 mg/dL.

rejected_candidates.text: not all favorable changes in serum lipid characteristics translate into a reduction in CVD events
rejected_candidates.reason: The text does not explicitly state that statins or any other drug reduce CVD events through changes in serum lipid characteristics.

rejected_candidates.text: Despite the overall lowering of mean LDL-C and the overall incidence of CVD in the US population, CVD remains a significant health burden and a leading cause of mortality.
rejected_candidates.reason: This statement is about the overall trend but does not provide specific drug-biomarker or drug-condition relationships.

rejected_candidates.text: One side of the debate advocates high dose statins while the other prefers combination therapy in moderate doses. The question is based in part on a balance between benefit and risk.
rejected_candidates.reason: This statement discusses the debate but does not provide explicit evidence for any drug-biomarker or drug-condition relationships.

rejected_candidates.text: These are addressed in this section in no particular order.
rejected_candidates.reason: This is an introductory statement and does not contain specific drug-biomarker or drug-condition relationships.
